content:
First, we rephrase the “no‐adjacent‐dominoes’’ condition in graph‐theoretic language.  Think of the $1000\times1000$ grid as the usual grid‐graph $G$ whose vertices are the unit‐cells and with edges joining side‐adjacent cells.  A domino covering cells $u,v$ is then just an edge $uv\in E(G)$.  Two dominoes are forbidden to be side‐adjacent if and only if their corresponding edges in $G$ have no edge between them in $G$.  In other words, the set of placed dominoes must form an induced matching in the grid‐graph.

A crude “area‐count’’ immediately gives an upper bound of $1000^2/8=125000$ dominoes, and one can actually attain that bound by a simple $4\times 4$–block pattern of density $2/16=1/8$.  

Here is the tiling:

Partition the $1000\times1000$ board into $250\times250$ disjoint $4\times4$‐blocks.  In each $4\times4$ block with local coordinates
\[
(i,j)\,,\quad i,j\in\{1,2,3,4\},
\]
place exactly two horizontal dominoes:

 • One domino on the cells $(2,1)$–$(2,2)$,  
 • Another domino on $(4,3)$–$(4,4)$.

Within a single $4\times4$ block these two dominoes clearly do not touch edge‐to‐edge, and one checks that when you place the same pattern in every block, no domino in one block ever becomes side‐adjacent to a domino in a neighbouring block (they end up at least two rows or two columns apart).  Thus this builds an induced matching of size
\[
\bigl(\#\text{blocks}\bigr)\times2 \;=\;(250\times250)\times2 \;=\;125000.
\]

Since the area‐count shows no larger induced matching is possible, the answer is

\boxed{125000}.
